food_subgroup: Cooked prawn dish with chilli-garlic sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general shrimp dish class (FOODON_03302051); no specific chilli garlic prawn entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked crustaceans broadly; no specific chilli garlic prawn code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for chilli garlic prawns as a prepared dish. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of chilli garlic prawns is approximately 250 g, comprising whole or halved prawns in a chilli-garlic sauce with spring onions, served as a main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, cooked (USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 175180) and USDA SR Legacy item 15152 — Crustaceans, shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat; adapted with sauce component estimates from USDA FoodData Central condiment and sauce entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for chilli garlic prawns exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180 / SR Legacy 15152) as the protein-fat-energy anchor, with carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, and fat adjusted upward to account for the chilli-garlic sauce (oil, soy sauce, chilli, garlic, spring onion). Sauce contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for soy sauce (FDC ID 172238) and garlic (FDC ID 169230). Composite estimate represents a typical restaurant-style chilli garlic prawn dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on cooked shrimp / seafood dish with sauce (low-carbohydrate protein-dominant food)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chilli garlic prawns. GI is estimated at 15 based on the analogous category of cooked seafood dishes with low-carbohydrate sauces, consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of protein-dominant seafood dishes as low GI (GI < 20). Carbohydrate per serving is approximately 10.5 g (4.2 g per 100 g × 250 g serving). GL = (15 × 10.5) / 100 = 1.6. Estimation basis: the dish is protein-dominant with minimal starch; sauce carbohydrates are primarily from small amounts of sugar and soy sauce.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and chef special sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 1300 mg per restaurant serving), which represents more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting a reduced-sauce or sauce-on-side preparation may help lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chilli garlic prawns have a low glycemic index (estimated GI ~15) and low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g), making them a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the high sodium content from the sauce warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and dietary cholesterol from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns are naturally higher in dietary cholesterol (~152 mg per 100 g) and the dish carries a high sodium load from the sauce; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation, preferably with a reduced-sauce preparation, and balance it with high-fibre, low-sodium foods in the same meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Garlic Prawns is a popular Indo-Chinese fusion seafood dish served at North Indian restaurants in Singapore, featuring whole or halved prawns stir-fried or sautéed in a bold sauce of red chilli, garlic, spring onion, and a chef's special sauce that typically incorporates soy sauce, oyster sauce, and aromatics. The dish delivers a fiery, pungent, and slightly sweet flavour profile with a tender-firm prawn texture and a glossy, clingy sauce. It is served as a main course, often accompanied by naan, roti, or steamed rice. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-rich (approximately 14.5 g protein per 100 g) and relatively low in carbohydrates (approximately 4.2 g per 100 g), giving it a low glycemic index estimated at around 15. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content, estimated at approximately 520 mg per 100 g, largely attributable to soy-based sauces, making it a dish that individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should consume in moderation. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low carbohydrate and low GI profile make it a favourable protein source when paired with low-GI accompaniments. The dish contains crustacean allergens and is not suitable for those with shellfish allergies. It is generally considered halal when prepared without alcohol-based ingredients.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for chilli garlic prawns exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data with sauce-component adjustments estimated from approved USDA source entries. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification framework for protein-dominant seafood dishes. Confidence is medium because the adaptation is methodologically sound but not based on a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) with upward adjustments to sodium, fat, and carbohydrate to account for the chilli-garlic sauce. These values should be replaced with direct laboratory analysis if this dish is to be used in a clinical or regulatory context. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp entry and is not expected to be significant in this dish. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA source entry used for this adaptation. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked shrimp and is not expected to be introduced by the sauce ingredients. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~15) based on the low-carbohydrate, protein-dominant nature of the dish, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classification principles for seafood dishes. 6. The dish is served at multiple North Indian restaurants in Singapore under the field survey platform; the food_name has been canonicalised by removing the promotional suffix 'Chef Recommendation'. 7. Halal status is assumed based on typical North Indian restaurant preparation in Singapore but cannot be guaranteed without restaurant-specific certification verification. 8. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp fatty acid profile and are approximate.
